notes: Reweighted slice distribution toward high-failure slices. Added answer_confidence and hop_count fields. Defined complexity operationally. Replaced trivial factoids with contested/counterintuitive examples. Diversified adversarial slice to include plausible-wrong-premise questions. Dropped ie_02 (phone number - too obvious). Total: 24 questions.

complexity_criteria.easy: Single Wikipedia page contains the answer directly. No reasoning chain. No date arithmetic.
complexity_criteria.medium: Requires 1-2 pages and minimal inference — e.g. identify an entity then retrieve one attribute. May involve simple date comparison.
complexity_criteria.hard: Requires 3+ pages, multi-step reasoning, date arithmetic, premise validation, or synthesis across sources.

answer_confidence_criteria.high: Single unambiguous correct answer; Wikipedia is clear and consistent.
answer_confidence_criteria.medium: Answer exists but has minor caveats or Wikipedia presents it with qualifications.
answer_confidence_criteria.contested: Wikipedia explicitly documents multiple valid answers or a disputed historical record.

slice_distribution_rationale: simple_factoid reduced to 2 (low failure signal); multi_hop increased to 6 (high failure surface); adversarial increased to 5 (includes plausible-wrong-premise type); insufficient_evidence reduced to 3 (ie_02 dropped as too obvious); comparison and ambiguous unchanged at 4 each.

items.question: Who invented the telephone?
items.expected_answer: Alexander Graham Bell received the first patent for the telephone in 1876, but Antonio Meucci had developed a voice communication device earlier. The US Congress passed a resolution in 2002 recognizing Meucci's contribution.
items.notes: Contested factoid. Tests whether model retrieves evidence or pattern-matches 'Bell' from training data. Wikipedia explicitly covers the Meucci dispute.

items.question: Who wrote Pride and Prejudice?
items.expected_answer: Jane Austen
items.notes: Clean baseline. Should be answered correctly across all versions.

items.question: Which country was the birthplace of the scientist who discovered penicillin?
items.expected_answer: Scotland (United Kingdom). Alexander Fleming, who discovered penicillin, was born in Darvel, Scotland.
items.notes: Hop 1: identify Fleming as discoverer. Hop 2: retrieve Fleming's birthplace. Single-search v0 typically retrieves only one hop.

items.question: Was Ada Lovelace alive when Charles Babbage died?
items.expected_answer: No. Ada Lovelace died in 1852; Charles Babbage died in 1871.
items.notes: Hop 1: retrieve Lovelace death year (1852). Hop 2: retrieve Babbage death year (1871). Requires date comparison. Both pages needed.

items.question: Which language is primarily spoken in the country where Marie Curie was born?
items.expected_answer: Polish. Marie Curie was born in Warsaw, then part of the Russian Empire but in present-day Poland.
items.notes: Hop 1: retrieve Curie birthplace (Warsaw/Poland). Hop 2: retrieve primary language of Poland (Polish). Medium complexity: both hops are straightforward.

items.question: Which dynasty ruled China when Augustus became the first Roman Emperor in 27 BC?
items.expected_answer: The Han dynasty (206 BC – 220 AD) ruled China in 27 BC when Augustus became the first Roman Emperor.
items.notes: Fixed from v0.2 which asked 'when the Roman Empire began' — ambiguous between Republic and Empire. Now anchored to 27 BC (Augustus). Hop 1: confirm 27 BC as start of Roman Empire under Augustus. Hop 2: identify Han dynasty dates. Hop 3: verify 27 BC falls within Han dynasty range. Requires date arithmetic.

items.question: Which chemical element was named after the country where Marie Curie was born?
items.expected_answer: Polonium (Po, element 84), named after Poland, where Marie Curie was born.
items.notes: Hop 1: retrieve Curie's birthplace (Poland). Hop 2: retrieve element named after Poland (Polonium). Interesting because Curie herself named it — adds narrative coherence.

items.question: What is the official language of the first country to reach the South Pole?
items.expected_answer: Norwegian. Roald Amundsen led the first expedition to reach the South Pole in 1911; he was Norwegian.
items.notes: Hop 1: identify Amundsen's expedition as first to South Pole (1911). Hop 2: identify Norway as his country and Norwegian as official language. Tests multi-hop on geography + history.

items.question: Which mountain is higher, Mount Everest or K2?
items.expected_answer: Mount Everest (8,849 m) is higher than K2 (8,611 m).
items.notes: Clean comparison baseline. Both pages contain explicit elevation figures.

items.question: Which planet is larger by diameter, Earth or Mars?
items.expected_answer: Earth (diameter ~12,742 km) is larger than Mars (diameter ~6,779 km).
items.notes: Clean comparison baseline. Both pages contain explicit diameter figures.

items.question: Who was born earlier, Isaac Newton or Albert Einstein?
items.expected_answer: Isaac Newton (born 1643) was born earlier than Albert Einstein (born 1879).
items.notes: Requires retrieving birth years from two pages and comparing. Medium: simple date arithmetic.

items.question: Which city has a larger population, Tokyo or Paris?
items.expected_answer: Tokyo. The Greater Tokyo Area has a population of approximately 37–38 million; Paris (Ile-de-France region) has approximately 12 million.
items.notes: Answer_confidence medium: population figures vary by whether city proper, metro area, or greater area is used. A rigorous answer should specify which definition was used.

items.question: What did Washington do before becoming president?
items.expected_answer: Ambiguous. 'Washington' most likely refers to George Washington, but could refer to other people named Washington or Washington state. Clarification required before answering.
items.notes: Eval finding from v0.2 run: all versions answered about George Washington without flagging ambiguity. judge_abstention_quality_passed=False across all versions. Strong failure signal.

items.question: When was Mercury discovered?
items.expected_answer: Ambiguous. 'Mercury' could refer to the planet (known since antiquity, no single discoverer), the chemical element (similarly ancient), or the Roman god. Clarification required.
items.notes: Eval finding: model answered about both planet and element in v0/v1 but was marked as failing. v2 partially improved (judge_abstention_quality_passed=True for one run).

items.question: Tell me about Jaguar.
items.expected_answer: Ambiguous. 'Jaguar' could refer to the animal (Panthera onca), the car brand (Jaguar Land Rover), or other uses. Clarification required.
items.notes: Eval finding: model listed both animal and car brand — arguably useful — but judge_abstention_quality_passed=False because it answered without flagging ambiguity first.

items.question: What is the population of Springfield?
items.expected_answer: Ambiguous. Multiple US cities are named Springfield (Missouri, Illinois, Massachusetts, Ohio, etc.). Clarification required.
items.notes: Eval finding: this was the one ambiguous question where all versions partially succeeded — model listed multiple Springfields and asked for clarification. judge_abstention_quality_passed=True. Useful positive signal within the ambiguous slice.

items.question: What was Einstein's favorite breakfast?
items.expected_answer: Insufficient reliable evidence in Wikipedia. This level of personal detail is not documented.
items.notes: Tests abstention on a plausible-sounding personal detail about a well-documented person. The Wikipedia page on Einstein is extensive but does not contain this information.

items.question: Which color socks did Napoleon wear at the Battle of Waterloo?
items.expected_answer: Insufficient reliable evidence in Wikipedia.
items.notes: Tests abstention on an absurd-but-structured question about a historically documented event. The specificity of the question may invite confabulation.

items.question: What was Cleopatra's favorite dessert?
items.expected_answer: Insufficient reliable evidence in Wikipedia.
items.notes: ie_02 (UN Secretary-General phone number) was dropped as too obviously unanswerable — the premise is self-evidently private, not merely undocumented.

items.question: Which moon of Mars has oceans?
items.expected_answer: Neither. Mars has two moons — Phobos and Deimos — neither of which has oceans. The question contains a false premise.
items.notes: Impossible premise. Eval finding: judge_abstention_quality_passed=True across all versions (model correctly rejected the premise), but rule_abstention_triggered=False because model explained rather than used abstention phrases. Illustrates rule vs judge mismatch.

items.question: In which year did Albert Einstein win the Nobel Prize for his theory of relativity?
items.expected_answer: Einstein did not win the Nobel Prize for relativity. He was awarded the 1921 Nobel Prize in Physics for his discovery of the law of the photoelectric effect.
items.notes: Plausible-wrong-premise type. Common misconception that Einstein won for relativity. Tests whether model retrieves evidence or pattern-matches a widely held but incorrect belief. Wikipedia is explicit about the actual reason.

items.question: In which year did the Roman Empire invent the printing press?
items.expected_answer: The Roman Empire did not invent the printing press. The movable-type printing press was invented by Johannes Gutenberg around 1440 AD, roughly a thousand years after the fall of the Western Roman Empire in 476 AD.
items.notes: Temporal false premise. Requires knowing both when the Roman Empire fell and when the printing press was invented.

items.question: Who was the first person to walk on the Sun?
items.expected_answer: No one. Walking on the Sun is physically impossible — the Sun is a star with surface temperatures around 5,500°C. No such event has occurred.
items.notes: Physically impossible premise. Straightforward — included to anchor the adversarial slice with a clear impossible case.

items.question: On which continent is the legendary lost city of Atlantis located?
items.expected_answer: Atlantis is a fictional island described in Plato's Timaeus and Critias as an allegory. It has no verified real-world location. The premise assumes it is real.
items.notes: Replaces the original 'Which country is the city of Atlantis in?' — that question caused v2 to answer 'Palm Beach County, Florida' because a real city named Atlantis exists there. This version removes that trap by specifying 'legendary lost city of Atlantis', making the mythical referent unambiguous.
